findings.title: Long method: build_app (755 lines)
findings.description: Function 'build_app' has 755 lines (effective threshold: 150).
findings.suggested_fix: Break into smaller, focused functions.
findings.why_it_matters: Long methods are hard to understand, test, and maintain. Each function should do one thing well.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 3 functions in call cycle (complexity 63)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 3 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/bat/src/bin/bat/main.rs::list_themes:196 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/bat/src/bin/bat/main.rs::run:345 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/bat/src/bin/bat/main.rs::run_controller:277. Aggregate complexity: 63.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Architectural Bottleneck: open (architectural hub)
findings.description:
Function 'open' is called by 22 functions and has complexity 26. Changes here are high-risk.

Betweenness centrality: 0.1915 - this function sits on many execution paths between other functions.

📊 Threshold: 20 (adaptive) | Actual: 26 | Default would be: 20 | Profile: p90=7, p95=13, mean=3.3, n=491
findings.suggested_fix: This is a critical hub. Ensure comprehensive test coverage, add defensive error handling, and consider circuit breaker pattern.
findings.why_it_matters: Bottlenecks are single points of failure that amplify bugs. High fan-in with high complexity means many callers depend on risky code.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 7 functions in call cycle (complexity 36)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 7 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/bat/src/vscreen.rs::impl<EscapeSequenceOffsetsIterator<'a>>::chars_take_while:371 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/bat/src/vscreen.rs::impl<EscapeSequenceOffsetsIterator<'a>>::next_csi:477 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/bat/src/vscreen.rs::impl<EscapeSequenceOffsetsIterator<'a>>::next_nf:510 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/bat/src/vscreen.rs::impl<EscapeSequenceOffsetsIterator<'a>>::next_osc:413 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/bat/src/vscreen.rs::impl<EscapeSequenceOffsetsIterator<'a>>::next_sequence:393 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/bat/src/vscreen.rs::impl<EscapeSequenceOffsetsIterator<'a>>::next_text:388 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/bat/src/vscreen.rs::impl<Iterator for EscapeSequenceOffsetsIterator<'_>>::next:544. Aggregate complexity: 36.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 2 functions in call cycle (complexity 31)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 2 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/bat/tests/benchmarks/highlighting-speed-src/jquery.js::mightThrow:3524 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/bat/tests/benchmarks/highlighting-speed-src/jquery.js::resolve:3520. Aggregate complexity: 31.
findings.suggested_fix: Consider refactoring to eliminate direct mutual calls. Common strategies: merge the two functions, use a callback parameter, or introduce a shared data structure that both functions operate on.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] This catch block silently swallows exceptions, hiding potential bugs.

**Risk Assessment:**
⚠️ `XMLHttpRequest` appears to do I/O
findings.suggested_fix:
This swallows I/O or network exceptions - very dangerous!
At minimum, log the exception:
```python
except Exception as e:
logger.error(f"Operation failed: {e}")
```
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

findings.title: Panic risk: unwrap() without context
findings.description: Using `unwrap()` can cause panics. Consider `?`, `unwrap_or`, or proper error handling.
findings.why_it_matters: Panics crash the program without recovery. Using proper error handling makes code more robust and debuggable.

findings.title: Callback hell (5 levels deep)
findings.description:
Deeply nested callbacks (5 levels) make code hard to follow.

**Analysis:**
✓ Uses Promise combinators (good pattern)
⚠️ 274 anonymous functions - consider naming them
findings.suggested_fix:
Refactor options:
1. Convert to async/await (recommended)
2. Extract nested callbacks into named functions
3. Use Promise.all() for parallel operations
findings.why_it_matters: The 'pyramid of doom' hurts readability and makes error handling difficult. Each nesting level increases cognitive load.

findings.title: Potential XSS vulnerability
findings.description: Direct HTML injection can lead to XSS attacks.
findings.suggested_fix: Sanitize input or use textContent instead.
findings.why_it_matters: XSS allows attackers to execute scripts in users' browsers.

findings.title: Potential command injection
findings.description: [Non-production path] subprocess with shell=True allows shell injection through any unsanitized input.
findings.suggested_fix: Use subprocess/spawn with array arguments instead of shell string. Never interpolate user input into commands.
findings.why_it_matters: Attackers could execute arbitrary system commands by injecting shell metacharacters.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 9 levels in to_tokens
findings.description:
9 levels of nesting (threshold: 7).

**Analysis:**
In function: `to_tokens`

Threshold: 5 (adaptive) | Actual: 9 | Default would be: 5 | Profile: p90=2, p95=3, mean=0.9, n=491
findings.suggested_fix: Extract nested logic into functions or use early returns.
findings.why_it_matters: Deep nesting makes code hard to read and maintain. Each level increases cognitive load exponentially.

findings.title: Long method: config (250 lines)
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Function 'config' has 250 lines (effective threshold: 150).

**Graph Analysis:**
High complexity density: 0.31 (complexity 78 / 250 lines)
Calls 3 different modules - possible split points
findings.suggested_fix:
This function calls 3 different modules. Consider extracting:
  - `handle_src()` for src operations
  - `handle_bat()` for bat operations
  - `handle_assets()` for assets operations
findings.why_it_matters: Long methods are hard to understand, test, and maintain. Each function should do one thing well.

findings.title: Circular dependency: 2 files
findings.description:
Mutual dependency between: decorations.rs ↔ printer.rs

**Weakest link to break:** `decorations.rs` → `printer.rs` (1 import)
findings.suggested_fix:
🔗 **Best place to break:** `decorations.rs` → `printer.rs` (weakest coupling: 1 imports)

Since `decorations.rs` only imports 1 symbol(s) from `printer.rs`, consider:
- Move those symbols to a shared module
- Pass them as parameters instead of importing
- Use dependency injection

**For small cycles:**
1. Merge tightly coupled modules if they're always used together
2. Use TYPE_CHECKING (Python) or type-only imports (TS) for type hints
3. Extract common code to a third module
findings.why_it_matters: Circular dependencies make code harder to understand, test, and maintain. They can cause import errors at runtime and make it difficult to refactor individual modules.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 9 levels in print_line
findings.description:
9 levels of nesting (threshold: 7).

**Analysis:**
In function: `print_line`
High complexity: 68 (nesting compounds this)
Contains 1 match/switch level(s) (discounted from raw depth 10)
Existing helpers that could reduce nesting: generate, char_width, highlight_regions_for_line

Threshold: 5 (adaptive) | Actual: 9 | Default would be: 5 | Profile: p90=2, p95=3, mean=0.9, n=491
findings.suggested_fix:
This function already has helpers like `generate`. Consider:
1. Extract more nested blocks into similar helpers
2. Use guard clauses (early returns) to reduce nesting
3. Replace nested ifs with switch/match
findings.why_it_matters: Deep nesting makes code hard to read and maintain. Each level increases cognitive load exponentially.

findings.title: Broad exception catch
findings.description:
Catching generic Exception hides bugs and makes debugging difficult.

**Analysis:**
📞 Try block calls: apply, call
findings.suggested_fix: Catch specific exceptions instead of generic Exception.
findings.why_it_matters: Broad exception catches mask unexpected errors like TypeErrors or AttributeErrors that indicate bugs in your code.

findings.title: Broad exception catch
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Catching generic Exception hides bugs and makes debugging difficult.

**Analysis:**
📞 Try block calls: getData
findings.suggested_fix:
Based on the operations in your try block, consider catching:
  • TypeError (network errors), AbortError

Example:
```python
except (TypeError (network errors), AbortError) as e:
logger.error(f"Operation failed: {e}")
```
findings.why_it_matters: Broad exception catches mask unexpected errors like TypeErrors or AttributeErrors that indicate bugs in your code.

findings.title: Broad exception catch
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Catching generic Exception hides bugs and makes debugging difficult.

**Analysis:**
📞 Try block calls: XMLHttpRequest
findings.suggested_fix:
Based on the operations in your try block, consider catching:
  • TypeError (network errors), AbortError

Example:
```python
except (TypeError (network errors), AbortError) as e:
logger.error(f"Operation failed: {e}")
```
findings.why_it_matters: Broad exception catches mask unexpected errors like TypeErrors or AttributeErrors that indicate bugs in your code.

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions
findings.description:
This catch block silently swallows exceptions, hiding potential bugs.

**Risk Assessment:**
⚠️ `close` appears to do I/O
findings.suggested_fix:
Log the exception or handle it appropriately:
- Add logging to track failures
- Re-raise if recovery isn't possible
- Handle specific exception types
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

findings.title: Loose equality (==) used
findings.description: == performs type coercion which can cause subtle bugs.
findings.why_it_matters:
Type coercion in == can cause unexpected behavior:
• '1' == 1 is true
• '' == false is true
• [] == false is true
Use === to compare both value AND type.

findings.title: Global mutable variable: test
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Global mutable state 'test' makes code hard to reason about.

**Impact Analysis:**
📊 Used 4 times in this file
findings.suggested_fix: Use const if immutable, or encapsulate in a module/class.
findings.why_it_matters: Global state causes hidden dependencies between functions. Changes to globals can have unexpected effects throughout the codebase.

findings.title: Global mutable variable: rain
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Global mutable state 'rain' makes code hard to reason about.

**Impact Analysis:**
📊 Used 9 times in this file
findings.suggested_fix:
Many usages - consider:
1. Encapsulate in a module with getter/setter
2. Convert to a class instance
3. Pass as parameter instead
findings.why_it_matters: Global state causes hidden dependencies between functions. Changes to globals can have unexpected effects throughout the codebase.

findings.title: Wildcard import: time
findings.description: Wildcard imports pollute namespace and hide dependencies.
findings.suggested_fix: Import specific names instead.
findings.why_it_matters: Makes code harder to understand and refactor. Tools can't determine where names come from.
